---
title: "Week 7: Interactions"
---

## Overview

This week introduces interaction effects, which allow the relationship between a predictor and the outcome to depend on the value of another variable. You will learn how to specify and interpret product terms for both continuous-by-categorical and continuous-by-continuous interactions, how to probe significant interactions using simple slopes and the Johnson-Neyman technique, and why centering predictors is critical before forming product terms.

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

- Construct and interpret product terms that represent interaction effects in a regression model
- Compute and interpret simple slopes at meaningful values of the moderator
- Apply the Johnson-Neyman technique to identify the region of significance for an interaction
- Distinguish between continuous-by-categorical and continuous-by-continuous interactions
- Create interaction plots that clearly communicate conditional relationships

## Readings

- {{< fa book-open >}} **Required**: [Chapter 5: Interactions]({{< var books.lecture_notes >}}/chapters/05-interactions.html){target="_blank"}

## R Lab

- {{< fa laptop-code >}} [Lab 06: Interactions]({{< var books.r_lab_book >}}/labs/lab06-interactions.html){target="_blank"}

## Homework

- {{< fa pen-ruler >}} [Homework 05](/assignment/hw05.qmd) — Due this week
- {{< fa pen-ruler >}} [Homework 06](/assignment/hw06.qmd) — Due this week

## Project

- {{< fa diagram-project >}} [Project 1 Proposal](/assignment/project-1-proposal.qmd) — Due this week

## Quiz

- {{< fa circle-question >}} **Quiz 3** — Covers Weeks 5--7 material

## Key Concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Interaction** | An effect in which the relationship between a predictor and the outcome varies across levels of another variable |
| **Product term** | The multiplicative combination of two predictors added to a model to estimate their interaction |
| **Simple slope** | The estimated effect of a focal predictor at a specific value of the moderator |
| **Johnson-Neyman technique** | A method that identifies the range of moderator values for which the focal predictor effect is statistically significant |
| **Moderation** | The conceptual framework in which one variable changes the strength or direction of another variable's effect |
| **Probing interactions** | Follow-up analyses, such as simple slopes or regions of significance, that clarify the nature of a detected interaction |
